title: Mud on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes plus drying time if wet mud is allowed to dry safely
stainChemistry: Mud on cotton is treated conservatively as dirt/dry soil with possible organic or protein components. The evidence supports gentle mechanical removal of loose dry soil and a cool-water detergent path, not one exact chemistry or routine oxidizer/hot-wash treatment.
whyThisWorks: Loose dry soil is best lifted mechanically before wet washing when it is safe and practical; cool back-rinsing and detergent then remove remaining water-soluble and suspended soil without grinding particles into the weave. Air-drying before heat confirms the mud is gone.
defaultAssumption: Use for washable cotton only. Let wet mud dry only when waiting is safe and practical; do not grind, scrub, or delay if the fabric is delicate, dye-unstable, or valuable.

spottingProtocol.technique: Care-label, construction, fiber, and colorfastness assessment
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cloth, hidden seam/hem test
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Stop immediately on transfer or change
spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm cotton is safe for wet treatment and document any Home attempts. Test colorfastness on a hidden seam or hem before any spotting.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only intake. Follow care label; reject or escalate unstable dye, structured construction, or high-value textiles.

spottingProtocol.technique: Use the mildest source-supported professional path first
spottingProtocol.equipment: Product-labeled detergent or family-specific spotter; clean towels
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Use product-label dwell only; stop on dye movement or texture change
spottingProtocol.instruction: Treat mud on cotton as a dry soil plus possible organic/protein component stain and begin with controlled detergent/water action before any stronger chemistry. Keep chemistry bounded by SDS/label and fabric test results.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only. Use PPE/ventilation required by product SDS; do not expose Home users to pro solvents, acids, oxidizers, or concentrated spotters.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush between products and inspect dry
spottingProtocol.equipment: Water/steam only if fabric and product label permit; drying rack
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Until residues are flushed and fabric is dry enough to inspect
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush or rinse out chemistry before switching products. Air-dry/inspect before considering any heat, oxidizer, solvent, or additional professional escalation.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only. Do not mix incompatible chemistry; use PPE/ventilation and document any last-resort escalation.

steps.dwellTime: Stop immediately on transfer or change

products: Product-labeled professional detergent/soil-removal systems only, used per SDS/label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cool first; any warmer professional laundering only after care-label and colorfastness checks
warnings: Pro-only escalation requires SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, and fabric testing. Avoid routine oxidizer layering and heat-first treatment.

homeSolutions.agent: Care Label and Practical Drying Check
homeSolutions.instruction: Check the care label. If wet mud can safely wait, let it dry so loose soil can be lifted. If waiting risks dye movement, odor, or damage, skip to cool back-rinsing and escalate if uncertain.
Shake outside or use a soft brush/spoon edge to lift loose dry mud. Work lightly and avoid grinding particles into the cotton.
Rinse from the back with cool water if the care label allows washing. Do not start with hot water and do not scrub.
Apply a small amount of generic liquid laundry detergent. For colored cotton, test on a hidden seam with a clean white cloth first and stop if dye transfers.
Wash according to the care label. Air-dry and inspect before any dryer or iron.

If soil remains but is improving, repeat the conservative loose-soil/detergent path once. Escalate for embedded mud, abrasion/fuzzing, dye transfer, ring formation, structured items, or no improvement after two rounds.

steps: 1. Care Label and Practical Drying Check: Check the care label. If wet mud can safely wait, let it dry so loose soil can be lifted. If waiting risks dye movement, odor, or damage, skip to cool back-rinsing and escalate if uncertain.
2. Gently Lift Loose Dry Soil: Shake outside or use a soft brush/spoon edge to lift loose dry mud. Work lightly and avoid grinding particles into the cotton.
3. Cool Back-Rinse if Washable: Rinse from the back with cool water if the care label allows washing. Do not start with hot water and do not scrub.
4. Generic Liquid Detergent: Apply a small amount of generic liquid laundry detergent. For colored cotton, test on a hidden seam with a clean white cloth first and stop if dye transfers.
5. Care-Label Wash and Air-Dry: Wash according to the care label. Air-dry and inspect before any dryer or iron.

6. Repeat Once or Escalate: If soil remains but is improving, repeat the conservative loose-soil/detergent path once. Escalate for embedded mud, abrasion/fuzzing, dye transfer, ring formation, structured items, or no improvement after two rounds.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on embedded mud that will not lift, dye transfer, abrasion/fuzzing, ring formation, dry-clean-only/structured items, or no improvement after two conservative rounds.
warnings: Do not grind mud into cotton
Cool water first for possible organic/protein components
No routine Home oxidizer, peroxide, branded oxygen bleach, hot wash, or pro chemistry

neverDo: Never put the item in a dryer, use an iron, or apply heat until the stain is gone after air-drying inspection
Never rub or scrub aggressively; blot, lift, or gently work only enough to avoid driving soil deeper
Never mix cleaning agents, bleach, ammonia, acids, solvents, or professional spotters
Never use chlorine bleach unless the care label allows it and the fabric is white or confirmed colorfast
Never use professional-strength oxidizers, acids, dry-side solvents, rust/mineral removers, adhesive removers, or named pro products in the Home path
Never grind or scrub mud into the weave
Never use hot water as the first step for mud with possible organic/protein components
Never layer oxidizers or use branded oxygen bleach/peroxide as a routine Home mud step
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is cellulose; white cotton may tolerate more laundering than colored cotton
Colored cotton requires a hidden seam dye-transfer/colorfastness test and lower-temperature caution
chemicalBoundaries: Home path is limited to water, generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent, and care-label laundering
Use any detergent, enzyme detergent, or pretreat only according to its product label and only after a hidden-area test on colored fabric
Oxidizers are not routine Home steps; any trace-color escalation must be care-label-safe, white/colorfast, hidden-test-passed, and never mixed with other cleaners
Professional chemistry is Pro-only and requires SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, colorfastness testing, and flush/rinse controls
Home mud path is mechanical loose-soil removal plus generic detergent/care-label wash only
No routine Home oxidizer, peroxide, branded oxygen bleach, solvent, or professional soil-removal chemistry
chemicalControls: Home path is limited to water, generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent, and care-label laundering
Use any detergent, enzyme detergent, or pretreat only according to its product label and only after a hidden-area test on colored fabric
Oxidizers are not routine Home steps; any trace-color escalation must be care-label-safe, white/colorfast, hidden-test-passed, and never mixed with other cleaners
Professional chemistry is Pro-only and requires SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, colorfastness testing, and flush/rinse controls
Home mud path is mechanical loose-soil removal plus generic detergent/care-label wash only
No routine Home oxidizer, peroxide, branded oxygen bleach, solvent, or professional soil-removal chemistry
homeAllowed: Cool/cold water or care-label-safe lukewarm water
Generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent
proOnly: Professional spotters, dry-side solvents, acids, oxidizers, reducers, concentrated chemistry, steam, and heat escalation require SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, flush/rinse controls, and documented fabric testing
testFirst: Test detergent, warmer water, enzyme detergent, oxidizer escalation, alcohol/solvent, acid spotter, or any pro chemistry on a hidden seam/hem with a clean white cloth before treating the visible area
safetyMatrix.colorfastnessTest: On a hidden seam or hem, press a clean white cloth with the planned detergent/water mix for one minute; stop if dye transfers, color changes, or texture changes.
safetyMatrix.flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse or flush out each cleaning agent before changing products; air-dry fully and inspect before any dryer, iron, heat, or professional escalation.
safetyMatrix.temperatureLimits: Cool/cold first for mud because it can include organic/protein components; use warmer water only if care label allows after loose soil is removed and stain is improving.
escalationTriggers: Stop and escalate on dye bleed, color loss, texture change, ring formation, spreading halo, or fabric distortion
Stop and escalate dry-clean-only, non-washable, lined, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or unstable-dye items
Escalate embedded mud, dye transfer, abrasion/fuzzing, ring formation, structured/dry-clean-only items, or no improvement after two rounds
stopAndEscalate: Stop and escalate on dye bleed, color loss, texture change, ring formation, spreading halo, or fabric distortion
Stop and escalate dry-clean-only, non-washable, lined, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or unstable-dye items
Escalate embedded mud, dye transfer, abrasion/fuzzing, ring formation, structured/dry-clean-only items, or no improvement after two rounds
safetyMatrix.ppeVentilation: For Home detergent use, follow the product label and keep products away from children and pets. For concentrates or Pro chemistry, use gloves/eye protection as the SDS requires and work with ventilation.
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home guidance excludes professional-strength oxidizers, acids, solvents, named pro products, exact pro concentrations, steam, and heat escalation; those belong only in a professional workflow with SDS/label controls.

materialWarnings: Colored cotton needs hidden-area dye-transfer testing
No dryer/iron until air-dry inspection shows mud is gone

escalation.when: Stop on embedded mud that will not lift, dye transfer, abrasion/fuzzing, ring formation, dry-clean-only/structured items, or no improvement after two conservative rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: Mud on cotton; whether it was wet or dried before treatment; whether loose soil was lifted; detergent used; any dye transfer, abrasion, ring, or remaining embedded soil.
escalation.specialistType: professional textile cleaner or dry cleaner

contextQuestions: Is the mud still wet or already dry?
Is the cotton white or colored?
Has anyone scrubbed it or put it in the dryer?
Is there embedded grit, fuzzing, or a ring?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Use dry-soil-first language conservatively; do not claim wetting is always wrong.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Consumer stain-family guidance for protein, tannin, oil, combination and unknown stains; blot rather than rub, work from the wrong side where appropriate, test first, and do not iron or dry until the stain is removed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Safety and process gates: heat, concentration, oxidation, abrasion and mismanaged water can damage textiles; detergents/surfactants suspend oily soils; test hidden areas, follow product labels, ventilate, use PPE for concentrates, avoid incompatible mixes, and clean only according to care label or reject/escalate.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Internal cotton/linen safety boundaries; protein, oil, tannin and oxidizer controls; dry-side solvent/pro chemistry boundaries; no hot water on protein stains; never mix chlorine bleach with ammonia or acids.

sources.title: R.R. Street 4Streets On-the-Spot
sources.evidence: Professional classification and operator workflow context for food/beverage, oil, tannin, protein and combination stains; pro-only product taxonomy.
